---
title: "Module 7: Some Claude Tips"
subtitle: "MGMT 675: Generative AI for Finance"
author: "Kerry Back, Rice University"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, revealjs-style.scss]
    highlight-style: monokai
    slide-number: c
    transition: fade
    navigation-mode: linear
    width: 1920
    height: 1080
    margin: 0.05
    center: true
    include-after-body:
      - text: |
          <script src="https://hypothes.is/embed.js" async></script>
---

## What We've Done

- AI + code tool
  - financial analysis (mean-variance, dcf*)
  - deliverables (figures, excel, word, powerpoint)
  - small web apps (artifacts, pyodide)
- Skills (reusable instructions)

## Where We're Going

:::{.three-cards}
:::{.card .card-light}
[**M7: Some Tips (Today)**]{.card-title}

- Final draft, not first draft
- Enhance AI memory
- Boris Cherny's tips
:::

:::{.card .card-light}
[**M8: Web Scraping with AI**]{.card-title}

- Scraping SEC EDGAR, news, transcripts
- Static vs. dynamic pages
- Magnolia example
:::

:::{.card .card-light}
[**M9: Build & Deploy Agents**]{.card-title}

- The API: software connections to LLMs 
- Chatbots and web apps
- Connecting tools
- FastAPI and deployment
:::
:::

## Where We're Going (cont.)

:::{.three-cards}
:::{.card .card-light}
[**M10: Corporate Deployment**]{.card-title}

- Data security: on-prem or cloud
- Guardrails and governance
:::

:::{.card .card-light}
[**M11: Retrieval Augmented Generation**]{.card-title}

- How RAG works
- Chunking, embedding, and vector search
- Magnolia example*

:::

:::{.card .card-light}
[**M12: AI Sentiment Analysis**]{.card-title}

- LLMs as sentiment classifiers
- News and earnings call analysis
- Trading signals from text
:::
:::

## Final Draft, Not First Draft {.section-divider}

## Let AI Revise Its Own Work

Don't review AI's [first draft]{.amber}. Instead:

1. Ask AI to produce the work
2. Ask it to [critique itself]{.amber} and revise
3. Repeat until no further revisions are needed
4. **Now** review what it produced

You review a [final draft]{.amber} instead of a first draft. This saves your time (though it burns tokens). The more consequential the work, the more revision cycles it deserves.

## The /critique Skill

The `/critique` skill automates this. It spawns [three parallel agents]{.amber}, each with a different lens, then synthesizes findings and applies revisions.

:::{.three-cards}
:::{.card .card-light}
[**Correctness & Completeness**]{.card-title}

Are there factual errors, logical gaps, or missing information? Are claims supported and assumptions justified?
:::

:::{.card .card-light}
[**Clarity & Persuasiveness**]{.card-title}

Is the structure logical? Is anything confusing, redundant, or buried? Could the main message be stated more directly?
:::

:::{.card .card-light}
[**Devil's Advocate**]{.card-title}

What are the strongest counterarguments? Where is the reasoning weakest? What would a skeptic challenge?
:::
:::

Download the [critique skill](/files/critique-skill.zip){target="_blank"} and ask Claude to install it.

## Enhance AI Memory {.section-divider}

## Saving Conversation Summaries {.shrink}

AI conversations have [no memory of previous conversations]{.amber} --- each new session starts from scratch. You can fix this by saving things to [markdown (.md) files]{.amber}.

**A summarize chat skill**

1. Download [summarize-chat skill](/files/summarize-chat-skill.zip){target="_blank"} and ask Claude to install it.
2. At the end of a chat, type `/summarize-chat`
3. The summary is saved as a `.md` file in a `chats/` folder.  Each session, Claude reads the most recent summaries on startup.

**Standard memory devices**

- **CLAUDE.md** --- project-level instructions loaded every session
- **Skills (.md)** --- reusable task-specific instructions
- **Other files** --- you can always ask Claude to save any chat or part of a chat as a file

## Markdown Quick Reference {.shrink}

Markdown is a plain-text format that uses simple symbols for formatting. AI reads and writes it natively.

```markdown
# Heading
## Subheading

- Bullet point
- **Bold text**
- *Italic text*

1. Numbered list
2. Second item

> Blockquote
```

## Markdown in AI Workflows

**Where you'll see it**

- **CLAUDE.md** --- project instructions
- **SKILL.md** --- skill definitions
- **Session summaries** --- conversation logs
- **README.md** --- any GitHub repo

You can read any markdown file in Notepad, but you get a better presentation with a markdown renderer, for example, [markdownlivepreview.com](https://markdownlivepreview.com){target="_blank"}.

## Claude Dispatch {.section-divider}

## Dispatch: Setup

Claude **Dispatch** lets you send a task from the Claude mobile app and have it [run on your desktop]{.amber} --- as either a Cowork or Code session, depending on the task.

**Setup**

1. [Update both apps first:]{.amber} Claude Desktop (Help → Check for Updates) and Claude mobile app (App Store / Google Play)
2. Open **Claude Desktop** → **Cowork** tab
3. Click **Dispatch** in the sidebar
4. Scan the QR code from your phone
5. Send a task --- e.g., *"fix the auth bug"*

## Dispatch: How It Works

Dispatch decides whether the task needs a Code session (dev work) or can be handled in Cowork (research, writing).

- Your desktop must be **awake and running** Claude Desktop
- Dev tasks → Code session spawned automatically
- You get a **push notification** when done or when approval is needed
- Full access to local files, MCP servers, and project config

[Requires Pro or Max plan.]{.amber}

## Boris Cherny's Tips {.section-divider}

Boris Cherny is the **creator and Head of Claude Code** at Anthropic. In a series of X posts over several weeks, he offered tips on how he and his group use Claude Code.

The tips were compiled by a fan and made available online [here](https://howborisusesclaudecode.com/){target="_blank"} --- dozens of tips organized into 10 parts covering workflows, memory, automation, and more.

**A few highlights:**

- Run [multiple tasks simultaneously]{.amber} instead of waiting for one to finish
- Set up [automatic quality checks]{.amber} (e.g., verify data after every change)
- Write detailed [CLAUDE.md]{.amber} files --- better instructions mean less repetition

His tips can be **installed as a Claude Code skill** --- paste the URL in Claude Code and ask it to create a skill from the content.

## Claude Plugins {.section-divider}

## What Are Plugins?

A **plugin** bundles multiple Claude Code components into a [single installable package]{.amber}:

| Component | What it does |
|---|---|
| Skills | Slash commands & auto-triggered prompts |
| Agents | Custom sub-agent definitions |
| Hooks | Event handlers (e.g., lint on file write) |
| MCP servers | External tool integrations |
| LSP servers | Language intelligence (type checking, etc.) |
| Output styles | Custom formatting for agent output |

## Installing and Sharing Plugins

:::{.two-col}
::::{.column}
**Installing**

- `/plugin install` from a marketplace or git repo
- Commands are [namespaced]{.amber}: `/my-plugin:hello`
- Versioned via `plugin.json` manifest

::::

::::{.column}
**Why plugins?**

- **Skills** are great for personal, single-project use
- **Plugins** are designed for [team distribution]{.amber} and cross-project reuse
- Available from the official marketplace, team marketplaces, or any git repo
- Browse community plugins: [anthropics/claude-plugins-official](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-plugins-official/tree/main/external_plugins){target="_blank"}
::::
:::

## The SaaSpocalypse {.shrink}

In early 2026, AI agent and plugin announcements triggered a [historic selloff]{.amber} in enterprise software stocks --- roughly [~$2 trillion]{.amber} in SaaS market cap evaporated between January and March. Wall Street dubbed it the [SaaSpocalypse]{.amber}.

AI is attacking the [interface and workflow layer, not the data layer]{.amber}:

:::{.three-cards}
:::{.card .card-light}
[**Replacing the UI**]{.card-title}

Many SaaS products are forms, dashboards, and screens sitting on top of databases. AI agents let users skip navigating 20 screens and just state what they want in natural language.
:::

:::{.card .card-light}
[**Automating Workflows**]{.card-title}

Task tracking, status updates, if-then automations, data transfer between systems --- exactly what agents do natively.
:::

:::{.card .card-light}
[**Collapsing Per-Seat Pricing**]{.card-title}

If 10 AI agents do the work of 100 human reps, you need [10 Salesforce seats, not 100]{.amber}. The revenue model collapses even if the product is still used.
:::
:::

## What Survives? {.shrink}

AI isn't replacing the database --- it's replacing the [screens people use to interact with it]{.amber}, and the humans who sit behind those screens.

:::{.two-col}
::::{.column}
**Defensible positions**

- [Systems of record]{.amber} in regulated industries (finance, healthcare, law)
- Platforms with accumulated [proprietary data]{.amber} and feedback loops
- Integration hubs managing connections across many systems
::::

::::{.column}
**The market damage (as of March 2026)**

- IGV (software ETF) down [~25% YTD]{.amber}
- Forward P/E compressed from ~34x to ~22x
- Software traded at a [discount to the S&P 500]{.amber} on a forward P/E basis --- a first
- Salesforce -31%, Adobe -32%, Atlassian -35%
::::
:::

[Sources: TechCrunch, CNBC, Bloomberg, SaaStr; data as of mid-March 2026.]{style="font-size: 0.7em; color: gray;"}

## Version Control with Git {.section-divider}

## Why Git Matters for AI Workflows {.shrink}

**Git** is like [Track Changes]{.amber} for an entire folder --- it saves every version so you can always go back.

:::{.two-col}
::::{.column}
**Why it matters with Claude**

1. **Safety net** --- it is much safer to let Claude edit your files when you can [easily roll back]{.amber} to any previous version
2. **Claude does the work** --- Claude can commit changes, review past commits, and recover old versions for you --- no need to learn git commands yourself
::::

::::{.column}
**Getting started**

Ask Claude to:

1. **Set up Git** --- ask Claude; it will install and configure it if needed
2. **Initialize a folder** as a git repo
3. **Commit changes** whenever you reach a good stopping point

That's it. Claude handles the commands. You just say [*"commit this"*]{.amber} or [*"show me what changed"*]{.amber}.
::::
:::

## Back Up to GitHub

Git keeps snapshots [locally]{.amber}. For an external backup, push to [GitHub]{.amber} (free account).

:::{.two-col}
::::{.column}
**Setup (one time)**

1. Create a free account at [github.com](https://github.com){target="_blank"}
2. Ask Claude to **install the GitHub CLI** (`gh`)
3. Run `gh auth login` --- it opens your browser to sign in (no tokens or passwords to manage)
4. Ask Claude to create a GitHub repo and push your folder to it

::::

::::{.column}
**Ongoing**

- After committing locally, say [*"push to GitHub"*]{.amber}
- Your work is now backed up in the cloud
- You can also browse history, compare versions, and restore files from the GitHub website
::::
:::
